lessons.title: The Mindset: Find the Cause by Evidence, Not Guessing
lessons.content:
Your car won't start. The natural urge is to poke at random: jiggle a wire, try the key again, maybe kick a tire. That **panic-poking** wastes time and often makes things worse. There is a calmer, faster way.

The core idea of **troubleshooting** (the act of finding why something is broken so you can fix it) is simple: a **fault** (something not working as it should) always has a *logical cause*. Nothing breaks by magic. Your job is to find that cause by **evidence** -- real observations -- not by guessing. Think of a detective: they follow clues to the culprit instead of accusing people at random.

Two habits sit at the heart of this mindset. First, **reproduce the problem**: can you make it happen on demand? A problem you can trigger reliably is far easier to fix than an **intermittent** one (a problem that comes and goes). So note its exact conditions -- when does it happen, and when does it *not*? "The engine only stalls when it's cold" is a huge clue, because it tells you where to look.

Second, **check the simple stuff first**. Before assuming something exotic is wrong, confirm the obvious: Is it plugged in? Is it switched on? Is there fuel, power, water? This is the famous "have you tried turning it off and on again?" -- not a joke, but the cheapest first test.

And resist the temptation to change five things at once. If you do, and the problem goes away, you won't know which change fixed it.

**Recap:** a fault has a logical cause; find it with evidence, not random pokes. Reproduce it, note its exact conditions, and check the simple stuff first.

items.prompt: What is the core mindset of good troubleshooting?
choices: A fault has a logical cause that you find by gathering evidence
Faults are mostly random, so just keep changing things until one works
The first thing you suspect is almost always correct
If a problem comes and goes, ignore it rather than study it
items.explanation: Good troubleshooting assumes every fault has a logical cause that evidence can reveal, like a detective following clues. Changing things at random is panic-poking, trusting your first suspect ignores evidence, and ignoring an on-and-off problem throws away the clues hidden in its conditions.

items.prompt: Your lamp won't turn on. Which response best fits the troubleshooting mindset?
choices: Buy a whole new lamp immediately without checking anything
Calmly check the obvious first, like whether it is plugged in and switched on
Rewire the lamp, change the bulb, and move it to a new room all at once
Decide it is probably the wiring in the wall and stop looking further
items.explanation: Checking the simple stuff first -- plug, switch, power -- is the cheapest, fastest test and fits the calm, evidence-first mindset. Buying a new lamp skips finding the cause, changing many things at once tangles cause and effect, and committing to the wall wiring with no evidence is guessing.

items.prompt: Why is it usually a mistake to change five things at once when trying to fix a problem?
choices: Because changing things is always forbidden during troubleshooting
Because it is slower than doing nothing at all
Because if the problem goes away, you won't know which change actually fixed it
Because the more changes you make, the more reliable the result becomes
items.explanation: If several changes happen together and the fault disappears, you cannot tell which one was responsible, so you have learned nothing you can trust or repeat. Changing things is allowed -- just one at a time; it is not slower than inaction; and piling on changes makes results less clear, not more reliable.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'reproduce' a problem?
choices: To make the problem happen on demand so you can study it
To photocopy the instructions that came with the device
To replace the broken item with an identical new one
To describe the problem to a friend and leave it at that
items.explanation: Reproducing means being able to trigger the problem whenever you want, which lets you observe it and test fixes. It is not about copying paperwork, swapping in a new item, or merely talking about the issue; the point is to make the fault appear reliably so you can examine it.

items.prompt: A car 'only stalls when the engine is cold.' Why is noticing that condition so valuable?
choices: It proves the car can never be fixed
It is irrelevant, since the conditions of a fault don't matter
It means you should ignore the stalling until it happens randomly
It is a strong clue that narrows down where the cause could be
items.explanation: Knowing the exact condition under which a fault appears points you toward its cause and rules out a lot of possibilities. The condition does not make the car unfixable, conditions matter a great deal, and ignoring the problem wastes the very clue that 'only when cold' hands you.

items.prompt: Why is a reliably reproducible problem usually easier to fix than an intermittent one?
choices: Because reproducible problems are always less serious
Because you can trigger it whenever you want, so you can observe it and test fixes
Because intermittent problems fix themselves if you wait
Because a reproducible problem needs no evidence to solve
items.explanation: If you can make the fault happen on demand, you can watch exactly what occurs and immediately see whether a change helped. Reproducible does not mean less serious, intermittent faults rarely cure themselves, and you still gather evidence -- being reproducible just makes that evidence far easier to collect.

items.prompt: Your Wi-Fi is down. Which action best reflects 'check the simple stuff first'?
choices: Immediately call an engineer to rewire your whole house
Assume your internet provider has permanently failed and give up
Confirm the router is powered on and the cables are plugged in, then try restarting it
Change your router, your laptop, and your provider all on the same day
items.explanation: Confirming power and connections and trying a restart are the cheapest, most common fixes, so they come first. Calling for a rewire skips the obvious checks, assuming a permanent provider failure is a guess with no evidence, and changing everything at once makes it impossible to know what helped.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best captures the troubleshooting mindset?
choices: A detective following clues to the culprit, not accusing people at random
A gambler hoping the next random guess pays off
A shopper buying replacements until something works
A person turning away from a problem because it is confusing
items.explanation: Troubleshooting is detective work: you let evidence lead you to the real cause rather than accusing at random. Gambling on guesses, buying replacements blindly, and avoiding the problem all abandon the patient, clue-following approach that finds faults fast.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'It's broken sometimes and works other times, so there's no point trying to figure out when -- I'll just keep poking at it.' What is the better approach?
choices: Agree, since an on-and-off problem can't be studied
Change as many parts as possible at once to cover all bases
Note exactly when it works and when it fails, to turn the random fault into a clue
Trust the very first cause that comes to mind and act on it
items.explanation: Recording when an intermittent fault appears and when it doesn't turns a frustrating on-and-off problem into useful evidence about its cause. An intermittent fault can absolutely be studied, changing many parts at once tangles cause and effect, and seizing the first theory you think of is guessing rather than gathering evidence.

lessons.title: Divide and Conquer: Isolate the Fault One Change at a Time
lessons.content:
Suppose the lamp still won't light. The suspects form a chain: the **bulb**, the **lamp** itself, the **outlet** (the wall socket), and the **breaker** (the household switch that cuts power to a circuit). Checking each one blindly is slow. There is a much faster technique.

It is called **divide and conquer** (also "isolating the fault"): split the system in half and test which half holds the problem, then split that half again. Each test roughly halves the number of suspects, so you zero in fast instead of inspecting everything. Computer scientists call this idea a **binary search**; in plain words, it is the high-low guessing game -- each guess cuts the remaining possibilities in half.

The most powerful tool for this is **known-good substitution**: swap in a part you *trust* to test a suspect. Put a bulb you know works into the dead lamp. If it lights, the old bulb was the fault. If it stays dark, the bulb was fine -- cross it off and move further down the chain. Plug another device into the same outlet: if that device works, the outlet is fine, so suspicion shifts back to the lamp.

Tying it all together is one golden rule: **change one thing at a time, and observe**. Make a single change, watch its effect, and keep a simple record of what you tried and what happened. If you tweak several things together, you tangle cause and effect and can't tell what mattered. A cook testing a recipe changes one ingredient per batch for exactly this reason.

**Recap:** divide the system in half to isolate the fault, use a known-good part to test each suspect, and change just one thing at a time so every result has a clear meaning.

items.prompt: What is 'divide and conquer' (isolating the fault) in troubleshooting?
choices: Replacing every part of the system at the same time
Splitting the system in half to find which half holds the fault, then splitting again
Asking someone else to deal with the whole problem
Guessing the cause and buying that part right away
items.explanation: Divide and conquer means halving the system, testing which half contains the fault, and repeating, so the suspects shrink fast. Replacing everything at once is the opposite of isolating, handing it off avoids the technique, and guessing-then-buying skips the systematic narrowing that makes this method powerful.

items.prompt: Why does halving the system on each test help you find a fault quickly?
choices: Because it removes the need to observe anything
Because changing many parts at once is always faster
Because each test roughly cuts the number of suspects in half
Because the fault is always exactly in the middle
items.explanation: Each halving test eliminates about half the remaining suspects, so you converge on the culprit in just a few steps. It does not remove the need to observe results, changing many parts at once tangles cause and effect, and the fault need not sit in the middle -- the test simply tells you which half to keep searching.

items.prompt: What is 'known-good substitution'?
choices: Throwing away any part you suspect without testing it
Swapping in a part you trust to test whether the suspect part is the fault
Buying the most expensive replacement part available
Changing several parts together to save time
items.explanation: Known-good substitution means putting in a part you are confident works to see whether the suspect was really to blame. Discarding parts untested wastes good components, price has nothing to do with it, and swapping several parts together would tangle cause and effect rather than isolate the one at fault.

items.prompt: You put a bulb you KNOW works into the dead lamp and it still won't light. What have you learned?
choices: The bulb was the problem all along
Nothing useful can be learned from this test
The bulb is fine, so you should look further down the chain (lamp, outlet, breaker)
You should now change the bulb, the lamp, and the outlet at once
items.explanation: A trusted bulb that still won't light clears the bulb of blame and points you onward to the lamp, outlet, or breaker. It cannot mean the original bulb was at fault, the test is genuinely informative, and changing three things at once would destroy the clarity this single substitution just gave you.

items.prompt: Why is 'change one thing at a time, and observe' such an important rule?
choices: Because making one change at a time means you never have to keep records
Because it guarantees the very first change will fix the problem
Because only one change is ever allowed for the entire repair
Because then each result has a clear meaning -- you can tell exactly what your change did
items.explanation: Changing a single thing and watching the effect lets you attribute the result to that one change, keeping cause and effect untangled. It does not remove the value of keeping records, it does not promise the first try works, and it does not limit you to one change total -- you make many changes, just one at a time.

items.prompt: A cook changes only one ingredient per batch when testing a recipe. Which troubleshooting habit does this match?
choices: Changing one thing at a time so you can tell what caused the difference
Guessing the result without tasting anything
Fixing the plate's appearance instead of the flavor
Ignoring batches that turn out inconsistent
items.explanation: Altering a single ingredient per batch is exactly change-one-thing-at-a-time: it isolates the effect of that ingredient. Skipping the taste is guessing without evidence, fixing appearance over flavor is treating a symptom, and ignoring inconsistent batches throws away clues -- none of which is what the careful cook is doing.

items.prompt: The high-low guessing game -- where each guess cuts the remaining numbers in half -- is a plain-words picture of which technique?
choices: Changing many variables at once
Divide and conquer (binary search) to isolate the fault
Fixing the symptom rather than the cause
Trusting your first theory without testing
items.explanation: Halving the possibilities with each guess is the binary-search idea, which is exactly how divide and conquer isolates a fault. It is not about changing many variables together, not about patching symptoms, and not about clinging to a first guess; it is systematic narrowing, one informative test at a time.

items.prompt: Your phone won't charge. Using known-good substitution, what is a strong first test?
choices: Replace the phone, the cable, and the wall charger all at the same time
Try a charging cable you know works, changing only that one part
Decide the phone's battery is dead and stop investigating
Charge it in five different rooms before changing anything
items.explanation: Swapping in a cable you trust -- and only the cable -- tests one suspect cleanly: if it charges, the old cable was the fault. Replacing everything at once tangles the result, declaring the battery dead is a guess with no evidence, and trying many rooms changes nothing about the suspected parts.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'Let's just swap the bulb, the cord, and the outlet together -- one of them is bound to be it.' Why is this weaker than divide and conquer?
choices: It isn't weaker; changing everything at once is the fastest method
Because the bulb is always the real cause anyway
Because even if it works, you won't know which part was actually at fault
Because you should never replace a part during troubleshooting
items.explanation: Swapping several parts together may get the lamp working, but it leaves you unable to say which part was broken -- so cause and effect stay tangled. Changing everything at once is not the fastest reliable method, the bulb is not automatically the cause, and replacing parts is fine when you do it one at a time to isolate the fault.

lessons.title: Test Cheapest First, Fix the Root Cause, Then Verify
lessons.content:
You have a method; now make it efficient and trap-proof. A **hypothesis** is just an educated guess at the cause. The smart order is to test the **most likely and cheapest-to-check** hypotheses first. For a flopped recipe, "maybe I misread the oven temperature" is quick to check, so test it before tearing the oven apart. If a guess is wrong, cross it off and move on. Crucially, **don't fall in love with your first theory** -- the urge to keep defending an early guess even as evidence piles up against it is called **confirmation bias**, and it is the classic way smart people waste hours. A good doctor rules causes out one by one; so should you.

Now the deepest idea: fix the **root cause**, not the symptom. The **symptom** is the visible effect; the **root cause** is the real underlying reason behind it. Mopping a puddle treats the symptom; the puddle returns until you fix the leaking pipe -- the root cause. A simple tool to dig down is the **"5 whys"**: keep asking *why* until you reach the real reason. Floor is wet -- why? A pipe drips -- why? A joint is loose. Tighten the joint and the puddle is gone for good.

Finally, two endgame habits that beginners skip. **Verify the fix**: confirm the problem is actually gone *and* that you didn't break something else. And **don't leave failed changes in**: if a change didn't help, undo it, or you leave a mess that confuses the next round.

**Recap:** test the cheapest likely causes first, avoid confirmation bias, fix the root cause rather than the symptom (use the 5 whys), then verify the fix and undo anything that didn't help.

items.prompt: When you have several possible causes (hypotheses), which should you usually test first?
choices: The most likely and cheapest-to-check cause
The most expensive and time-consuming one, to get it over with
Whichever one you find most interesting
All of them at once, so you don't have to think about order
items.explanation: Testing the most likely and cheapest causes first finds many faults quickly with the least effort. Starting with the costliest option wastes time, picking the most interesting ignores likelihood, and testing everything at once tangles cause and effect so you can't tell which guess was right.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a symptom and a root cause?
choices: They are two words for exactly the same thing
The symptom is the visible effect; the root cause is the real underlying reason behind it
The root cause is what you see; the symptom is hidden
The symptom is always more important to fix than the root cause
items.explanation: The symptom is the effect you notice, while the root cause is the deeper reason that keeps producing it. They are not the same thing, the labels are not reversed, and fixing only the symptom leaves the root cause to bring the problem back -- which is why the root cause is what you ultimately want to fix.

items.prompt: Mopping up a puddle again and again while the pipe keeps leaking is an example of what mistake?
choices: Reproducing the problem too carefully
Testing the cheapest hypothesis first
Fixing the symptom instead of the root cause
Changing only one thing at a time
items.explanation: Mopping clears the visible puddle (the symptom) but the leak (the root cause) refills it, so the fix never lasts. It is not over-careful reproduction, not cheap-first testing, and not single-variable changing -- it is the classic trap of treating the symptom while the real cause goes untouched.

items.prompt: What does the '5 whys' technique help you do?
choices: Decide how many people to blame for a problem
Keep asking 'why' until you reach the real underlying cause
Count exactly five separate problems before fixing any
Prove that the first cause you thought of was right
items.explanation: The 5 whys means repeatedly asking why -- floor wet, pipe drips, joint loose -- until you hit the root cause you can actually fix. It is not about assigning blame, it is not a rule to find exactly five problems, and it is meant to dig past your first guess rather than confirm it.

items.prompt: What is 'confirmation bias' in troubleshooting?
choices: Carefully writing down each test and its result
Checking the simple, obvious causes before the complicated ones
Splitting the system in half to isolate the fault
Clinging to your first theory and ignoring evidence that it's wrong
items.explanation: Confirmation bias is favoring your first guess and brushing aside evidence against it, which can waste hours chasing the wrong cause. Recording tests, checking simple causes first, and dividing the system are all sound habits -- the trap is falling in love with an early theory rather than following the evidence.

items.prompt: After you think you've fixed a problem, why should you verify the fix?
choices: To confirm the problem is truly gone and that you didn't break something else
Because verifying is only needed if someone is watching
To make the repair take as long as possible
Because a fix you believe in never needs checking
items.explanation: Verifying confirms the fault is actually gone and that your change didn't cause a new problem, which is the only way to know you're really done. Verification isn't for show, it isn't about stalling, and confidence is not proof -- even a fix you trust must be checked against reality.

items.prompt: You tried a change, it didn't help, and you left it in place anyway before trying the next idea. Why is leaving failed changes in a trap?
choices: It isn't a problem; leftover changes never cause confusion
Because leftover changes pile up and confuse the next round of testing
Because undoing a change is against the rules of troubleshooting
Because the more leftover changes there are, the clearer the cause becomes
items.explanation: Failed changes left in place stack up and muddy later tests, so you can no longer tell what is affecting the system. Leftover changes do cause confusion, undoing them is encouraged rather than forbidden, and more clutter makes the cause harder to see, not clearer.

items.prompt: A recipe flopped. Which approach best fits 'test the cheapest likely cause first and avoid confirmation bias'?
choices: Insist it must be a bad oven and replace the oven before anything else
Quickly recheck the oven temperature and your measurements, and be ready to drop a guess that proves wrong
Assume your favorite theory is right and stop looking once you've named it
Change the recipe, the oven, and the pan all at once and hope
items.explanation: Rechecking temperature and measurements is fast and likely, and staying ready to abandon a wrong guess is the cure for confirmation bias. Replacing the oven first is expensive and unproven, locking onto a favorite theory is the bias itself, and changing everything at once tangles cause and effect.

items.prompt: Which sequence best summarizes calm, systematic troubleshooting from start to finish?
choices: Guess a cause, defend it no matter what, and ship the repair without checking
Change many things at once, fix the symptom, and skip verifying
Reproduce it, gather evidence, divide and conquer, change one thing at a time, fix the root cause, then verify
Ignore the conditions, mop the symptom, and leave failed changes in place
items.explanation: The full method is reproduce, gather evidence, divide and conquer to isolate, change one thing at a time, fix the root cause, and verify -- each step building on real observations. The other options bundle the classic traps: defending a first guess, changing many things at once, patching symptoms, ignoring conditions, and skipping verification.
